mappings.rationale: Directly covers the crusading movement from the First Crusade (1096) through the later crusades, the crusader states, and the decline of the crusading movement.
Directly relevant to subsections A.1.c and A.3 on the Byzantine Empire from the Comneni through the Palaeologus dynasty and the fall of Constantinople (1453).
Relevant to subsection B on the Islamic states of Southwest Asia that interacted with and resisted the Crusaders.
Directly relevant to subsection B covering the Seljuqs, Ayyubids, Mamluks, Almoravids, Almohads, and the development of Islamic states in Southwest Asia and North Africa.
Directly relevant to subsection A.2 on the Mongolian invasions of eastern Europe and Southwest Asia and the Mongol successor states including the Golden Horde and Il Khans.
Directly relevant to subsections A.1.e and A.2.a on Russia from the Kievan state through the Mongol invasion, Tatar rule, and the rise of Muscovy under Ivan III.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Eastern Christendom, the schism with Rome, the Fourth Crusade's sack of Constantinople, and the Palaeologus dynasty's relations with the papacy.
Provides broader context for the crusading movement within the framework of medieval European society and the expansion of Western Christendom.
Relevant to subsection B.4.c on Almohad patronage of philosophy and arts, and the intellectual culture of Islamic states during this period.
Relevant to the section's coverage of Islamic intellectual life in the states that preceded and coexisted with the Crusader presence.
Directly relevant to the section's discussion of the disruption of pilgrimage routes by Muslims as a background cause of the First Crusade.
Relevant to the section's coverage of the spread of Islam across Southwest Asia, North Africa, and Europe through the various dynasties and empires discussed.
Directly relevant to the crusading movement as a quintessential example of religiously motivated warfare between Christianity and Islam.
Relevant to the crusading ethos of religious sacrifice and the concept of holy war (jihad) on both Christian and Muslim sides.
Relevant to the section's extensive coverage of ruling dynasties — Seljuqs, Ayyubids, Mamluks, Ottomans, Palaeologus, and others.
The section deals extensively with historical sources and the interpretation of the crusading era across multiple civilizational perspectives.
